title: U.S. Coast Guard Year in Review
description: For the Coast Guard, 2025 was a year like no other: organizational transformation, historic investment in people and platforms, and significant operational outcomes.
text:
For the Coast Guard, 2025 was a year like no other: organizational transformation, historic investment in people and platforms, and significant operational outcomes. To borrow from Star Trek, it has been change at warp speed.
Admiral Lunday Takes Command
On 19 November 2025, then–Acting Commandant of the Coast Guard Admiral
Kevin Lunday appeared before the Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation
Committee for his nomination hearing. In his testimony, he noted the need for a stronger, more capable Coast Guard:
For the past nine months, I have led the Coast Guard . . . to control,
secure, and defend the U.S. border and maritime approaches; facilitate and secure commerce . . .; and ensure the service is ready to successfully respond to a crisis or contingency that may come without warning. . . .
I’m filled with hope for the future of the Coast Guard because no matter the challenges ahead, we will prevail. If there is one thing I know—and our service history proves—with a ready Coast Guard crew and the strong support of the American people, there is nothing we can’t accomplish.1
Admiral Lunday was confirmed by the Senate on 18 December and assumed command as the 28th Commandant of the Coast Guard on 19 January 2026.
Force Design 2028
The most fundamental change for the Coast Guard came via Force Design 2028 (FD28), a generational reorganization and refocusing of the service. Bolstered by $24 billion in funding to kickstart recapitalization of aging assets and infrastructure, FD28 seeks to prepare the service to meet evolving challenges. Its initiatives are divided into four major campaigns: Organization, People, Technology, and Contracting and Acquisition.
Organization. Most changes are targeted at the headquarters level. Within mission support, FD28 finalizes the transition to a Deputy Commandant for Personnel and Deputy Commandant for Systems. In recognition of long-standing acquisition and support hurdles, five program executive offices (PEOs) will be established under the Systems organization: surface; aviation; shore; command, control, communications, computers, cyber, and intelligence (C5I); and robotics and autonomous systems.
Unlike current life-cycle management—which is split between the Assistant Commandant for Capabilities, who manages requirements; the Assistant Commandant for Acquisitions; and asset-level service centers with class-level product lines that manage major maintenance—the PEOs will maintain cradle-to-grave capability management within a single entity for each asset type to improve efficiency and accountability. Balancing the Systems overhaul, the Deputy Commandant for Personnel will focus on recruitment and training, workforce management, family and employee support, and healthcare.
People. FD28 aims to grow the service by 15,000 members over the next three years, supported by at least six new recruiting offices in addition to the 10 opened since 2023. Investments in enlisted and officer training centers and the development of a new civilian career progression framework will support recruiting and retaining the best talent. Replacing the decades-old training management tool with the Competence and Operational Baseline Readiness Application was a significant step toward modernizing personnel systems to better support the workforce.
On the operationally focused side of headquarters, major changes included creating a Chief of Staff and an Assistant Commandant for Operational Integration and Response Policy (CG-3/5R), after years of not having a CG-3 office. Other changes will include establishing a Deployable Specialized Forces Command (reminiscent of the former Deployable Operations Group), bolstering Coast Guard Cyber Command, and ensuring the Coast Guard is prepared for major global contingency operations.
Finally, 2025 saw the establishment of a long-overdue Futures Development and Integration Directorate, which will bring together program analysis and evaluation, the Deputy Commandant for Emerging Policy, and the Coast Guard Research and Development Center (RDC) under a single, forward-looking enterprise. Similar in concept to the Marine Corps Warfighting Lab and Futures Directorate, the new directorate gives the Coast Guard a one-stop shop for policy, strategy, research, wargaming, innovation, and analysis.
Technology. FD28 leans heavily into technology to improve efficiency, situational awareness, and operational effectiveness. Knowing that you cannot respond to what you do not know is happening, the service is creating a next-generation maritime surveillance capability. Coast Sentinel will provide a network of sensors and technologies to collect, process, and analyze information, using artificial intelligence when appropriate, to speed delivery of actionable intelligence to decision-makers.
Supporting this vision are other complementary efforts, including shifting to model-based systems engineering for the Coast Guard’s IT network, revamping public-facing applications such as those used in mariner credentialing and licensing, and establishing a rapid prototyping team (RAPTOR) to get leading technology into the hands of operators prior to embarking on long-term acquisitions efforts. The RDC’s alignment with the Futures Development and Integration Directorate already has led to collaboration opportunities with RAPTOR.
A prime example of this forward lean on technology is the Coast Guard’s Operational Demonstrations Coqui I and II, during which the service integrated advanced RDC technologies in front-line operations with cutters and several partner agencies.2 Employing multiple uncrewed systems, including prototype uncrewed boats and uncrewed aircraft capable of operating beyond visual line of sight, the monthlong Coqui demonstrations focused on maritime security near western Puerto Rico, in the U.S. Virgin Islands, and on the northern border.
During the Puerto Rico and U.S. Virgin Islands operations, the data collected was fed back to Coast Guard command-and-control centers as well as Customs and Border Protection’s Air and Marine Operation Center in California. A milestone of more than five years of research into unmanned systems, the successful operationalization of these advanced technologies earned the RDC the Federal Laboratory Consortium’s technology transfer award for 2025.
Contracting and Acquisition. The service also saw a historic investment in upgrades and maintenance in 2025: $24.6 billion in appropriations. Roughly $9.6 billion will fund new cutters and systems, while $4.4 billion will support badly needed shore facility upgrades. Aircraft programs, also in need of additional funding, received a boost, with $3.4 billion for procurement, simulators, and training.
Drug and Alien Interdiction
The Coast Guard seized more than $2.2 billion in illegal narcotics in fiscal year 2025, including a record-breaking 510,000 pounds of cocaine, more than three times its annual average. Multiple interdiction efforts supported this effort.
Operation Border Trident, led by Coast Guard District Southwest, used an interagency approach to detection, monitoring, interdiction, and apprehension operations to combat transnational criminal organization and illegal migration activity in the California coastal region. The Alameda, California–based USCGC Bertholf (WMSL-750) was a centerpiece of this operation, conducting 86 security boardings and queries in the vicinity of San Diego, checking IDs, and inspecting closed cabin vessels to thwart illegal activity. This included more than 250 crew hours deployed in the Bertholf’s response boats, providing law enforcement presence and deterrence.
The USCGC Hamilton (WMSL-753) made history during Operation Pacific Viper, an operation focused on drug cartels and transnational criminal organizations in the eastern Pacific. In August, the Hamilton recorded the largest single drug offload in service history, with more than 76,000 pounds of cocaine and marijuana. Less than two months later, the operation, which involves extensive coordination with international and interagency partners, including Joint Interagency Task Force South, eclipsed the 100,000-pound mark for seizures.
“Alongside our partners and allies, our maritime fighting force is scouring drug smuggling routes in the eastern Pacific and dismantling narcoterrorist networks. . . . As we mark our interdiction of 100,000 pounds, we are already working towards the next milestone,” said Rear Admiral Jeffrey Novak, Deputy Commander of Coast Guard Pacific Area.3
Other drug interdiction highlights included the USCGC Stone’s (WMSL-758) offload of more than 45,000 pounds of narcotics, valued at more than $500 million, in March; a 21,000-pound, $156 million offload by the Midgett (WMSL-757) in September; a 19,000-pound, $140 million offload by the Calhoun (WMSL-759) in May; and a 17,000-pound, $132 million offload by the Valiant (WMEC-621) in June.
Marking Coast Guard aviation’s continuing contributions to the interdiction mission, in August, the Helicopter Interdiction Tactical Squadron (HITRON) recorded its 1,000th interdiction of a suspected narcotrafficking vessel since the unit’s inception in 1999.
“From its humble beginnings as a prototype program, [HITRON] has evolved into a vital force in our counternarcotics efforts. This milestone is a powerful reminder of the strategic value of this specialized unit in addressing the evolving complex maritime security challenges,” Vice Admiral Nathan Moore, Commander, U.S. Coast Guard Atlantic Area, remarked.4
Alien interdiction operations also remained a focus. The long-running interagency Operation Vigilant Sentry maintains eyes on maritime alien movement in the Caribbean that may be bound for the United States. By September, the Coast Guard had interdicted and repatriated more than 600 Haitians, among others from various Caribbean nations.
To address an administrative gap in existing Coast Guard data systems, in June the service implemented the Alien Interdiction Operations Tracking Tool as the authoritative database to track maritime alien encounters, cases, chain of custody, and other relevant information for aliens and smugglers encountered during its operations.
In October, the Coast Guard launched Operation River Wall, bringing the service’s tactical boat experience in offshore, coastal, and riverine environments to the Rio Grande River. Working with partners in the U.S. Border Patrol and at U.S. Northern Command, these efforts are focused on preventing unauthorized crossings along the eastern 260 miles of the river and include assets from the Coast Guard’s deployable specialized forces.
Fisheries Enforcement
The Coast Guard aggressively pursued and interdicted foreign fishing vessels encroaching along the maritime boundary between Mexico and Texas, with the fishermen detained and turned over to U.S. Homeland Security for processing and prosecution.
In a high-profile case in September, crews from Station South Padre Island, supported by Coast Guard Air Station Corpus Christi, interdicted a vessel with four fishermen on board and seized more than 450 pounds of fish that had been illegally caught in U.S. waters. Through December, more than 50 fishermen from Mexico had been detained, and each could face up to five years in prison and fines of $250,000.
The U.S. Department of the Treasury’s 2024 sanctioning of five individuals associated with a Mexican drug cartel who were engaged in illegal, unreported, and unregulated (IUU) fishing highlights the link between the lanchas and the cartels and paved the way for the increased enforcement posture.5
Marine Safety
Marine safety missions are the essence of the service’s “flow of commerce” value proposition, especially to the extent they enable the Coast Guard to control, secure, and defend U.S. ports and waterways. The executive order “Restoring America’s Maritime Dominance” aims to revitalize the nation’s shipbuilding and maritime operational capabilities, including mariner training and education, the U.S. Merchant Marine Academy and state maritime academies, and mariner licensing and credentialing, among other focus areas. FD28’s emphasis on revamping the systems that support the maritime industry, including registration and credentialing systems, appears to be directly related to the executive order.
In July, a final rule on cybersecurity in the marine transportation system went into effect. The new regulations recognize the growing risk cybersecurity vulnerabilities pose to the nation’s maritime system and are some of the most notable updates to vessel security requirements since the Maritime Transportation Security Act was passed in 2002. The final rule requires vessels to develop and maintain a cybersecurity plan and a cyber incident response plan; designate a cybersecurity officer; and undertake certain actions to maintain cybersecurity, such as account and device security measures. It also establishes reporting requirements for cybersecurity incidents.
In the vein of new threats within the marine safety mission, in June the Coast Guard responded to the M/V Morning Midas, a 600-foot car carrier that suffered an onboard fire while sailing near Adak, Alaska. The vessel was carrying nearly 3,000 cars, and the fire is suspected to have been started by a lithium-ion battery in one of the vehicles. Crews out of Air Stations Kodiak and Adak were deployed to the scene, and a nearby merchant vessel rescued the 22 crew members before the Morning Midas sank in about 16,000 feet of water. This incident highlights the evolving safety challenges and risks within the maritime industry and the need to adapt response techniques and technologies to a changing landscape.
Search and Rescue and Responding in Crisis
The Coast Guard shines in times of crisis. Two major events in 2025 highlight members’ professionalism when conducting search-and-rescue missions in disaster areas: the July flooding in Texas and the inundation from Typhoon Halong in western Alaska.
In Kerrville, Texas, the rising waters of the Guadalupe River decimated nearby homes and camps. Flying more than 150 miles inland, an HH-65 crew from Air Station Corpus Christi responded to an assistance request from the state to help locate and rescue those threatened by flash flooding. Dropping its rescue swimmer to Camp Mystic, Rescue 6553 spent several hours airlifting children and counselors to safety while its rescue swimmer triaged and organized operations on scene. The 165 people Rescue 6553 airlifted from the camp were part of the 230 the Coast Guard eventually rescued in partnership with state and local agencies.6
Farther north, in October, the Coast Guard and National Guard came to the rescue of hundreds in western Alaska affected by Typhoon Halong and subsequent flooding. Coast Guard air crews from Air Station Kodiak rescued 34 people in Kwigillingok and Kipnuk after the towns were inundated by devastating floods. Air crews brought in critical medical supplies and personnel to the community and transferred to safety another two dozen residents who had taken temporary refuge in a shelter.
Maritime Security
The Arctic continues to be an area of tense international competition. The past year’s activities were in line with the growing presence of Chinese and Russian vessels in and around the U.S. exclusive economic zone. In September, the USCGC Healy (WAGB-20) was deployed to query and monitor the activities of the M/V Zhong Shan Da Xue Ji Di and M/V Ji Di, two Chinese research vessels operating north of Utqiagvik, Alaska, within the U.S. Arctic Extended Continental Shelf area. The Healy was supported by an HC-130 from Coast Guard Air Station Kodiak operating under Operation Frontier Sentinel, focused on foreign activity in U.S. waters.
“This operation highlights the value of our ice-capable fleet. The U.S. Coast Guard is controlling, securing, and defending the northern U.S. border and maritime approaches in the Arctic to protect U.S. sovereignty, and Healy’s operations demonstrate the critical need for more Coast Guard icebreakers to achieve that,” said Rear Admiral Bob Little, Commander, U.S. Coast Guard Arctic District.7
Ice Operations
At the other end of the globe, the USCGC Polar Star (WAGB-10) completed a 128-day deployment in support of Operation Deep Freeze, the annual break out of the McMurdo Research Station in Antarctica. This was the cutter’s 28th deployment in support of the National Science Foundation and the U.S. Antarctic Program. During the deployment, the Polar Star created a channel through 14 miles of ice up to six feet thick, allowing multiple international vessels to reach and help resupply the station for the coming year. Between the Healy in the north and the Polar Star in the south, the Coast Guard continued to demonstrate the importance of a strong, ice-capable fleet.
Assets and Acquisitions
As a testament to the criticality of ice operations, 2025 saw renewed focus and action on increasing the Coast Guard’s icebreaker fleet.
A multiyear process to acquire a commercial icebreaker for Coast Guard service culminated in August with commissioning of the USCGC Storis (WAGB-21). The Storis, previously the Arctic ice-breaking towing vessel Aiviq, completed her first patrol as a Coast Guard cutter in October, supporting Operation Frontier Sentinel and helping monitor Chinese research vessels Ji Di and Xue Long 2 in the U.S. Arctic.8
In addition, the White House pledged support for a new icebreaker acquisition program. While final numbers still are being worked out, the effort likely will entail smaller assets for domestic work in the northeast and on the Great Lakes, as well as additional medium and heavy icebreakers for polar operations. In a statement regarding the push for new assets, the Coast Guard said:
The Service must replace, modernize, and grow our fleet of icebreakers to safeguard our national security and economic prosperity that flows through America’s $5.4 trillion marine transportation system. We are working with the Administration and Congress to implement the President’s direction.9
In 2025, the Coast Guard also began construction of the new waterways commerce cutters (WCCs), sorely needed to recapitalize the aging inland fleet. The average age of the current inland river fleet is more than 57 years. Built by Birdon in Bayou La Batre, Alabama, the WCC fleet will help maintain commerce through more than 12,000 miles of waterways, supporting the transit of more than 630 million tons of cargo annually.
Bolstered by increased funding, the Coast Guard exercised an option to build ten additional fast response cutters (FRCs), bringing the fleet target to 77 hulls.10 The USCGC Earl Cunningham (WPC-1159) was commissioned in August, becoming the 59th FRC in service and the second of three to be homeported in Kodiak, Alaska. Designed as a replacement for the 110-foot Island class, the FRCs have proved far more capable, routinely pushing farther offshore to patrol and disrupt and interdict illicit activity.
“Since its introduction to the fleet in 2012 . . . the Fast Response Cutter has consistently proven its capabilities, adaptability, and effectiveness in a wide range of maritime environments and Coast Guard missions,” said Rear Admiral Mike Campbell, the Coast Guard’s Chief Acquisition Officer. The FRCs are being built by Bollinger Shipyards in Lockport, Louisiana.
Final items
The Coast Guard announced in November that it had achieved its highest recruitment numbers since 1991, bringing in 5,204 active-duty enlisted and exceeding the FY25 goal by just more than 900 members.11 In the officer corps, the news was equally good. The service commissioned 371 new officers, the highest number achieved during peacetime. Even the Coast Guard Reserve had a banner year. The goal was to reach 750 new members, and the service recruited 777. “More Americans want to serve in the Coast Guard than ever before,” noted Admiral Lunday.
The Coast Guard also renamed its districts in July, switching from numbers to geographic names that align with the districts’ areas of responsibility. District 1, for example, is now U.S. Coast Guard New England.
As the Coast Guard begins 2026 and the Force Design 2028 plan unfolds, the effect on the service will be significant. As the service-wide “ALCOAST” message noted, “This plan outlines how we WILL BE a stronger, more ready, and more capable fighting force.”
